name: ACE — Agentic Context Engineering
maintainer: SambaNova Systems, Stanford University, UC Berkeley (Qizheng Zhang, Changran Hu, Shubhangi Upasani, Boyuan Ma, Fenglu Hong, et al.)
last_major_release: [uncertain] — Initial open-source release on GitHub (ace-agent/ace); paper published October 2025 (arXiv 2510.04618). No versioned release tags confirmed beyond initial publication.
activity: GitHub repo ace-agent/ace: ~980 stars as of 2026-04. Multiple community forks (mgechev/ACE-open, JRay-Lin/ace-agents, kayba-ai/agentic-context-engine). Paper published October 2025; open-sourced shortly after via SambaNova blog. Moderate activity — research prototype stage.
storage_backend: Plaintext / file-based. ACE stores the evolving Playbook as a structured text document (markdown or similar). No graph database, vector DB, or SQL backend described. The Curator writes delta updates to the Playbook file; the Grow-and-Refine Scheduler manages file-level compaction. KV-cache reuse mentioned as an optimization component. No persistent external DB required.
representation_primitive: Episodic-note / structured-markdown Playbook. The core primitive is the Playbook — a structured repository of guidelines (strategies, heuristics, domain rules) that grows and changes over time. Content is organized as bullet points and sections within a text document. Delta updates are localized edits (add/modify/remove bullets) rather than full rewrites. Semantic embeddings are used for deduplication during merge, but no graph or entity-edge structure is maintained.
api_shape: Python library (ace-agent/ace SDK with Generator, Reflector, Curator services, Delta Context API, Curator Merge Engine, Grow-and-Refine Scheduler). No MCP server documented. No REST API. CLI scripts for offline and online benchmark runs (AppWorld, Finance). First-party MCP server: no.
hosting: Self-host only. Research prototype; no managed cloud service. SambaNova provides compute for their own benchmarks but no hosted ACE-as-a-service.
llm_dependency: Both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: Generator produces reasoning trajectories (LLM call); Reflector distills insights from successes/errors (LLM call); Curator integrates deltas into Playbook (partially LLM, partially deterministic merge). Retrieval: the Playbook is injected into the system prompt / agent context (no separate retrieval step — it is the context). Default model used in experiments: DeepSeek-V3.1 (open-source); compared against IBM-CUGA (GPT-4.1). LLM cannot be disabled — the pipeline is entirely LLM-driven.
schema_extensibility: LLM-learns (open discovery). The Playbook structure emerges from execution feedback without a predefined schema. The Reflector discovers what to track; the Curator decides how to organise it. No YAML config or fixed taxonomy. The grow-and-refine principle allows the schema to expand (new bullets/sections) and contract (dedup/merge) organically.
schema_richness: Not applicable in the traditional entity/edge sense. ACE has no graph schema. The Playbook is a flat-to-moderately-hierarchical text document with sections and bullet points. There are no defined entity types or edge types. Core types: Strategy bullet, Error pattern, Domain rule, Workflow step — all free-text. Multi-label: no. Multi-graph: no. Schema richness relative to Gimle §5 targets (20+ entities / 15+ edges): incompatible — ACE is a text-curation layer, not a graph store.
temporal_model: Event-log / append-then-refine. Delta updates are sequential: each execution episode appends new insights, and the Grow-and-Refine Scheduler periodically compacts/deduplicates the Playbook. No formal bi-temporal model. No valid_from/to + recorded_at. The Playbook captures a current-state view; historical deltas may be retained as an implicit log but are not queryable as a temporal dimension.
forgetting_policy: Semantic-dedup + agent-driven-deletion. The deduplication step prunes redundancy by comparing bullets via semantic embeddings. The Grow-and-Refine Scheduler controls context expansion versus compression. Explicit deletion: the Curator removes obsolete or contradicted bullets during merge. No LRU/LFU time-based eviction.
ingest_latency_class: Batch-offline (primary) + real-time-incremental (online mode). Offline mode: system prompts optimized over historical rollouts (minutes per batch). Online mode: after each execution episode, Reflector + Curator run synchronously to update the Playbook — adds latency per episode. ACE claims −91.5% latency vs Dynamic Cheatsheet in FiNER online scenario. Overall adaptation latency reduced 86.9% vs baselines.
retrieval_modes: No retrieval in the traditional sense. The Playbook is injected wholesale into the LLM context (system prompt or agent memory prefix). No vector search, BM25, graph-traversal, or hybrid retrieval. The retrieval is deterministic: the current Playbook is always fully included. This works because the Playbook is kept compact (grow-and-refine keeps it within context window budget).
retrieval_agenticity: Static-lookup (the Playbook is injected as-is). No HyDE, Self-RAG, CRAG, or multi-hop. The agentic intelligence is entirely in the Reflector's insight extraction and Curator's delta integration, not in retrieval routing. The Generator acts as the downstream agent using the context.
retrieval_budget_control: No explicit per-query token/hop/edge budget parameters. Implicitly, the Grow-and-Refine Scheduler enforces a Playbook length budget to keep it within the LLM context window. No user-facing budget API.
multi_project_namespace: None defined in the framework. ACE maintains a single Playbook per task domain (e.g., one for AppWorld agent, one for Finance agent). Multi-project use would require separate Playbook files per project, managed externally. No built-in namespacing or cross-project query support.
multi_user_access: None. ACE is a single-agent, single-Playbook system. No RBAC, no per-agent namespace beyond the Playbook file itself. Multi-agent use would require separate ACE instances.
embedder_options: [uncertain] Semantic embeddings used for deduplication (Curator Merge Engine). Specific embedding model not documented in the paper; likely uses the same LLM or a lightweight sentence-transformer. No built-in adapter registry for multiple embedders. Dimensions not specified.
llm_client_options: Paper uses DeepSeek-V3.1 for all roles (Generator, Reflector, Curator). Implementation likely supports any OpenAI-compatible API. No Anthropic or Ollama adapters explicitly mentioned. LLM cannot be disabled — the GRC pipeline requires LLM for all three roles.
cost_privacy: Data stays local (self-hosted). Token cost: Generator runs one full execution trajectory per episode; Reflector runs one reflection call; Curator runs one merge call — approximately 3x LLM calls per episode vs a baseline single-call agent. Paper reports −83.6% token cost reduction vs Dynamic Cheatsheet baseline in FiNER, and −86.9% latency reduction, suggesting the Playbook approach is net cheaper than repeated full-context rewriting. No data leaves the host except to the configured LLM API endpoint.
code_artifact_linkage: None. ACE is domain-agnostic; it has no concept of Symbol, File, Commit, PR, or code structure. The Playbook contains free-text strategies, not code-aware entities. Theoretically a Reflector could be prompted to extract code-related insights, but no first-party support exists. Ranked: none — ACE does not address code artifact linkage.
code_analysis_features: None. No call graph, diff-awareness, symbol index, dependency graph, import resolution, type-aware analysis, or cross-language support. ACE is a context-curation layer, not a code intelligence layer.
memory_os_layer: Plaintext only. ACE operates exclusively at the plaintext tier — the Playbook is a text document injected into LLM context. No activation (KV-cache) or parametric (weight tuning) tiers are addressed. Cross-type migration: not applicable.
self_improvement_mechanism: ACE-GRC (canonical implementation of this pattern). The Generator-Reflector-Curator pipeline is the defining self-improvement mechanism: Generator produces execution trajectories, Reflector extracts structured insights from successes/failures without labeled supervision, Curator integrates insights as incremental delta updates preventing context collapse. This is explicitly the ACE contribution — a novel GRC pipeline enabling schema self-improvement from execution feedback.
provenance_standard: None formally. Delta updates are sequential but no audit trail with W3C-PROV, OpenTelemetry, or MCP-native-trace is described. The Playbook evolution is implicitly trackable via git diff on the Playbook file. No structured provenance metadata per insight.
benchmark_results: AppWorld benchmark (agentic tasks): ACE boosts accuracy by up to +17.1% by learning from execution feedback without ground-truth labels. Matches top-ranked IBM-CUGA (GPT-4.1) on overall average; surpasses it on harder test-challenge split using DeepSeek-V3.1 (smaller open-source model). Finance domain (FiNER): +8.6% vs strong baselines. Agentic domain overall: +10.6% vs strong baselines. Latency: −91.5% vs Dynamic Cheatsheet; −86.9% adaptation latency reduction. Token cost: −83.6% rollout cost vs Dynamic Cheatsheet. No SWE-bench, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, or CrossCodeEval results reported.
strengths: 1. Schema self-improvement without labeled supervision: the GRC pipeline learns what to track purely from execution feedback — directly applicable to Gimle's need for schema self-improvement as agents execute tasks. 2. Context-collapse prevention via structured delta updates: incremental localized edits preserve accumulated knowledge across many iterations, solving the rewrite-erosion problem that plagues naive memory systems. 3. Proven cross-domain efficacy: +10.6% agents and +8.6% finance on strong baselines using a smaller open-source model, with dramatic cost/latency reductions — evidence the pattern generalizes beyond a single benchmark.
weaknesses: 1. No graph or structured storage: the Playbook is a text document — cannot represent Gimle's rich entity-edge schema (Symbol to File to Module to Project chain) or support graph-traversal queries. 2. No temporal or provenance model: insights are accumulated without timestamps, valid_from/to semantics, or audit trail — insufficient for Gimle's bi-temporal decision/iteration tracking. 3. Single-agent, single-domain scope: no multi-project namespace, no multi-user access, no cross-agent memory sharing — would need significant architectural extension for Gimle's multi-agent team context.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas — specifically the GRC pipeline pattern. Do not adopt whole — ACE has no graph schema, no code intelligence, no bi-temporal model. Ideas to steal: (A) GRC pipeline for schema self-improvement: implement a Reflector that, after each agent execution episode, extracts structured observations about which Gimle schema nodes/edges were most useful and emits delta updates to a schema-evolution log. The Curator then proposes additions to the canonical schema (new entity types, new edge types) for human review. Example delta format: {op: add_edge_type, from: Symbol, to: Decision, label: influenced_by, evidence: [run_id:abc, run_id:def]}. (B) Incremental delta updates instead of full-schema rewrites: when updating Gimle's project-level context (e.g., architecture notes, team conventions), use localized edits rather than full document replacement to prevent context collapse over long project lifetimes. (C) Grow-and-refine scheduler for context window budget: implement a compaction step that merges redundant schema notes and prunes obsolete guidelines when the injected context exceeds a token budget threshold. Incompatible aspects: ACE's flat Playbook cannot replace Gimle's Neo4j-backed entity-edge graph; it is a complement for unstructured guidelines, not a substitute for structured schema.
